Dear Mr. Hall-abrin:
The office 
of Statewide Health Planning and DevelopmenE is
currently responsible for the administration of a program to
provide foan insurance for e1i9ib1e health facility 
construction
loans. This program is found in Chapter 4 (commencj.nq with section
436) and Part 1 of Division 1 of the Heal-th and safety code.
It is within the cal-Mortgage Section of the oivi-sion of Facitities
Development and Finance that credit deci.sions are made regarding
short-term and long-tern taxable and tax-exempt debt instruments,
including mortgages, cer-'iflcates of partici.pation, charter city
revenue bonds, and health f aci-J-ity revenue bond issues. A decisj-on
to insure a project by the offj.ce affects the relative credit
worthj-ness of a heal-th facility 
construction project. 
A decision
to insure a project lends Cal-iforniars bond ratinq (AAA) to the
proposed debt instrument which results in a lower net interest cost
to the borrower than that which wou],d have resulted, had the health
f acj-J-ity been rated (by Moody's or Standard and Poors) entirely on
its own credit.
A decision to insure by this office also results in the bond issue
being backed by the fu1I faith and credit of the State of
California (see Heal-th and safety code, Secti-on 436.2o). fn short,
a default of an insured mortgage of bond issues causes that debt
instrument to become a direct obligation to the State of
Californj-a, a situation r./e are not interested in havinq occur j.f we
can help it.
The potential- exposure of the State to losses as a result of the
default of an insured project requires that each application for
insurance be thoroughly examined in every asPect-
In an effort to give each applicant close scrutiny for f j-nancial
viabj-lity, the office has created, at 22 ccR 91499, and I'Advisory
Loan con;ittee.ri 
The committee is appointed by our Director' 
It
meets in public, in accordance with the State's open meetj-ng law'
The text of our regulation establishes this committee as follows:
(i)


Mr. Scott Hallabri-n

91499 . Advisory Loan Committee.
(a) The Committee sha11 advise the Office regarding the
borrowerrs financial capability to service the 1oan.

consist of seven members as follows:
1. 
Five shaLl be appoj.nted outside state

interest code.

health facility 
in which such member has an economic
interest .

Members appointed forrn outside State covernment shafl be
entitled to cl-aim travel expenses equivalent to those
!'rhich State ernployees are entitled.
The current Advisory Loan Committee is made up of two government

The publj.c members are appoi.nted for five year terrns and, by

employed by a hospital as the Financial- Vice President and the

nexus sometirnes occurs in conjunction with agTenda iterns.
we have nade every effort to assure that a conflict of interest, or
even the appearance of a conflict, 
does not occur. I draw your
attention specifically to subdi.visions (d) and (e) of ccR Section
9L497. Subdivision (d) requires the filing of conf l-ict of interest
statements .
Subdivision (e) precludes deci-sion making or
participation j-n decision naking where an actual conflict exists-
It is the policy of the office and of the Advisory Loan committee
that, when a cbnfLi,ct of interest or a potential conf.Lict of
interest relating to a prospective action before the committee is
recognized, the member or members with confticting interest(s)
"ste6 downi and do not participate in the decision naking process
rela-tive to that itern. 
we know of no case where this has not
happened .


Mr. Scott Eallabrin
April 4, 1991

When members rrstep down,rr they oralIy state the nature of thej-r
conflicting interest(s) .
The Office retains through an Inter-Agency Agreement with the State

an Assistant Attorney ceneral who
attends loan committee meetings and advises the office and the
Advisory Loan Cornmittee regarding conflicts of interest.
The Office routinely advi-ses the Advisory Loan Committee in writing
of public policy and any other official 
requirements. Enctosed
with this request you will find policy and information letters sent

Statement of Economic Interest
"Annuai Statement"
The Political Reform Act of 197 4 requires that officials and employees who are
designated in the agency's conilict of interest code must file a Statement of
Economic Interests Form 730. These statements disclose certain financial interests
so the public may be aware of potential con-flicts of interests. Within in this Office,
your position is "designated" and the completion of a Form 730 is required. Your
disclosure category is indicated above.
Attached is a Statement of Economic Interests Manual and Form 730 . The period
covered by this arurual statement is January 1, 1990 through December 31, 1990.
Please complete the enclosed Form 730 and return it to my attention at the above
address no later than April 1, 1991. Also attached is a copy of the office's
disclosure categories which specifies the levels of disclosure.
Ifyou have anv questions, please contact me at (916) 327-9052 or ATSS 8-467-9052.

Dear Joan:
The Director has decj.ded to restructure the Advisory Loan
Connittee. His decision was based on the following observations:
(1) On average, only three of the five term menbers are able to
at+,end a qj.ven meeting and rernain for the duration. 
(2)
Applications to the prograrn have increased. (3) Meetings should be
held more freguentl-y. (4) Menbers sometimes disgualify themselves
due to conflicts of interest. (5) There is a need for a broader
array of ski11s to inprove the scope of review.
Here is the new structure:
* There will be five term nembers, two State Bero.bers, and two
alternate nembers .
* Each term member will .ce appointed to a five year term.
* Alternate and State mernbers will serve at the pleasure
of the director and are not subject to term appointnents.
Tems of terrn nembers shali be staggered so that each year
one term will expire on December 31st.
Alternates are requested :o attend each meeting and to ful-Iy
participate j.n the discussion and critique, but will not
be eligible to move, second, or vote.
Alternates roay be appointed by the chairman to replace an
absent tern or State member, in which case, the alternate
rnember shall have fuII rights to nove, second, and vote.
A najority of the pernanent and governrnent menbers consti-
tutes a quorun.
,::.,A.:
-dr
* Menbers nay be replaced for excessive absenteeism.

Dear Vince:
The next loan conmittee meeting is tentatively set for Thursday,
August 17th. Time and place to be determined after the agenda is
known.
our next meeting wi1l. be conpletel,y public lrith all discussions and
votes taken in the presence of applicants, consultants, and others
interested in our process. The neeting will be electronically
recorded and a secretary will be present.
Historicalty, 
committee members have been comfortable with an
inforrnaL meeting protocol resulting in a thorough review and frank
determination of financiaL feasibility. 
Through this process we
were able to protect proprietary information and probe project
weaknesses that might embarrass applicants if done pubIic1y.
The Baqley-Keene Act, Lrhich governs state agency advisory committee
rneetj,ngs of three or nore persons, is specific in its application
to our loan committee. The attached open Meetino La$rs pamphlet,
published by the california Attorney General's office, is enclosed
for your reading. It conveniently sunmarizes the Act by issue with
cross references to specific code sections (pp3-6). This should
expedite your research and understanding. 
PIease pay close
attention to the closed session exceptions (p4-5).
Here are sorne positive reasons why rre should welcome this change to
a public forun:
* rts government in the best of democratic traditions.
* It will encourage public testinony from interested parties
(pro and con), thereby increasing your awareness of comrnunj-ty
support and market conditions that nay not be apparent in the
feasibility 
study.
* Applicants will be nore precise in their asserti.ons and
more conservative in their assumptions.
* 
Potential applicants and their consultants wiII have a
better understanding of what you as a loan comrnittee member think
important and will respond by addressing these concerns in future
applications.
* Its the 1aw.
I cannot envision that the public neeting environment wil'l in any
way inhibit a thorough review of financial feasibility. 
You and
th-e other loan committee members are all experts in your field and
your opinions and criticisms are always relevent and on point.
barol uunter, Assistant Attorney General , has been invited to our
next meeting to answer any questions you may have about the Bag1ey-
Keene Act. In the meantime, call me if you have any questions or
concerns .


S incerely ,
Richard A. Mcl'tanus
chairman

sections.question: The Office would appreciate an opinion from the Fair Political Practices Commission as to the appropriateness and sufficiency of the procedures described above, as they relate to precluding conflicts of interest situations for Advisory Loan Committee members and the Office. Do we comport with all state statutory requirements for: 1) reporting financial interest so the public can make its own informed judgment regarding conflicts, and 2) prohibiting Advisory Loan Committee members from participating in decision making, when an actual conflict exists?

sections.question_synthetic: Does the Cal-Mortgage Loan Guarantee Program's Advisory Loan Committee comply with state conflict of interest statutes regarding financial interest disclosure and recusal procedures for committee members?
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is an incoming request letter with detailed context documents attached (memoranda, restructuring letter, meeting schedule, and form instructions). No FPPC response conclusion is present in this document. The question section is clearly identified in the main letter from Richard McManus.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The Office would appreciate an opinion from the Fair Political Practices Commission as to the appropriateness and sufficiency of the procedures described above, as they relate to precluding conflicts of interest situations for Advisory Loan Committee members and the Office. Do we comport with all state statutory requirements for: 1) reporting financial interest so the public can make its own informed judgment regarding conflicts, and 2) prohibiting Advisory Loan Committee members from participating in decision making, when an actual conflict exists?

embedding.summary: The California Office of Statewide Health Planning and Development requests FPPC advice on whether its Advisory Loan Committee's conflict of interest procedures comply with state statutory requirements for disclosure and recusal of members with economic interests. The request includes regulatory framework, committee composition, and supporting correspondence regarding conflict management practices.
